content.text: DevEx Surveys and Platform Feedback Loops
A Claude developer platform that optimises for what its team
developers need — rather than what they

experience — will invest engineering effort in the wrong places. DevEx surveys and structured feedback loops provide the signal that prevents this: they measure developer satisfaction, identify friction points that telemetry cannot surface, and create a visible improvement cycle that builds trust between the platform team and its consumers.

attrs.alt: DevEx feedback loop: Measure → Analyse → Act → Close the loop → next quarter

content.text: dimensions (how developers feel about the platform) and

dimensions (what they actually do). Surveys capture perception; telemetry captures behavior. Both are necessary — a team can be satisfied with a slow tool because they have adapted their workflow around it, and telemetry alone would not flag the dissatisfaction.
How likely are you to recommend the Claude platform to a colleague? (0–10)
How easy was it to get your first Claude integration running? (1–5)
How well does the platform documentation answer your questions? (1–5)
When you needed platform support, how quickly did you get a useful answer? (1–5)
Overall, how much effort does it take to get your Claude use case working? (1–5, lower is better)
Time-to-first-completion: minutes from API key creation to first successful Claude API call. Target: < 30 minutes.
SDK adoption rate: % of Claude-using services that import acme-claude-sdk vs. calling the API directly. Target: > 85%.
Template usage rate: % of new Claude projects bootstrapped from the Copier starter template. Target: > 70%.
p99 latency of acme-claude-sdk.complete(): measured weekly via synthetic probes. Target: < 4s.
Support ticket volume: tickets filed per 100 active Claude users per quarter. Target: < 5.

A poorly designed survey collects noise instead of signal. The following principles keep the quarterly DevEx survey short enough that engineers complete it, specific enough that the results are actionable, and consistent enough that trends are visible quarter-over-quarter.
: each additional question reduces completion rate by ~10%. Prioritise ruthlessly.
: never rephrase a recurring question — changes in wording invalidate trend comparisons.
: 'What is the single biggest pain point you have with the Claude platform right now?' The free-text responses yield the most actionable insights.
: link responses to team (not individual) so the platform team can segment by team size and use-case without creating psychological safety concerns.
Time it after a platform change
: send the survey 2–3 weeks after a significant platform improvement to measure perceived impact.

# survey_template.yaml  — quarterly DevEx survey structure
metadata:
  name: "Claude Platform DevEx Survey Q{quarter} {year}"
  cadence: quarterly
  max_questions: 7
  anonymity: team_level  # not individual

questions:
  - id: nps
    type: nps          # 0-10 scale, auto-calculates promoter/detractor
    text: "How likely are you to recommend the Claude developer platform to a colleague? (0 = not at all, 10 = extremely likely)"

  - id: csat_onboarding
    type: likert_5
    text: "How easy was it to get your first Claude integration working?"
    anchors: ["Very difficult", "Very easy"]

  - id: csat_docs
    type: likert_5
    text: "How well does the platform documentation answer your questions?"
    anchors: ["Not at all", "Completely"]

  - id: csat_support
    type: likert_5
    text: "When you needed platform help, how quickly did you receive a useful response?"
    anchors: ["Never / not useful", "Always quickly"]

  - id: effort_score
    type: likert_5
    text: "How much effort does it take to accomplish a typical Claude integration task?"
    anchors: ["Enormous effort", "Effortless"]
    reverse_scored: true  # lower = better

  - id: top_pain_point
    type: multiple_choice
    text: "Which area causes you the most friction? (pick one)"
    options:
      - "Getting API access / quota"
      - "Documentation and examples"
      - "SDK / library APIs"
      - "Debugging and observability"
      - "Cost visibility"
      - "Other (describe below)"

  - id: open_text
    type: text
    text: "What is the single biggest pain point you have with the Claude platform right now?"
    required: false

The most important — and most commonly skipped — step in the DevEx feedback loop is publicly communicating what changed as a result of the survey. Without this step, developers correctly conclude that the survey is performative and stop completing it. The
report is published within 30 days of the survey closing, lists the top five themes identified, and shows a concrete action taken in response to each.

# You Said, We Did — Q1 2025 Claude Platform DevEx Survey

## Survey Participation
- 87 engineers responded (64% response rate across 22 teams)
- NPS: 42 (up from 31 in Q4 2024)
- CSAT — Onboarding: 3.8 / 5 (up from 3.2)

## What You Said → What We Did

| Theme | Frequency | Action Taken | Status |
|-------|-----------|--------------|--------|
| "API key provisioning takes too long" | 34 responses | Self-service portal launched (< 2 min) | ✅ Done |
| "SDK docs lack runnable examples" | 28 responses | Added 12 copy-paste examples to SDK docs | ✅ Done |
| "No visibility into my team's spend" | 21 responses | Per-team cost dashboard in IDP | 🚧 In progress (Q2) |
| "Eval runner hard to configure locally" | 18 responses | New `acme-eval` CLI with guided setup | 🚧 In progress (Q2) |
| "Breaking SDK changes not announced" | 14 responses | 60-day notice policy + #platform-announcements | ✅ Done |

## What We're NOT Doing (and Why)
- "Provide a no-code Claude integration UI" — out of scope for platform team; routing to product teams.
- "Support GPT models through the same SDK" — conflicts with standardisation goal; declined.

## Next Survey: Q2 2025 (April)
We will specifically re-ask the top pain points above to measure whether our fixes landed.

captures qualitative signal continuously. Any engineer can add an entry to the friction log — a shared document accessible via the IDP — describing a moment where the platform made their work harder than it should have been. The log is reviewed by the platform team weekly; entries that appear three or more times from different teams become priority items for the next sprint.

<!-- friction-log.md  — pinned in #platform-claude-sdk Slack -->
## Friction Log — ongoing, reviewed weekly

| Date | Team | Area | Friction Point | Severity (1-3) |
|------|------|------|----------------|----------------|
| 2025-03-12 | search | SDK | `AcludeClaude.complete()` raises `AttributeError` on empty string prompt, not `ValueError` | 2 |
| 2025-03-14 | recommendations | Docs | Can't find example of streaming response parsing anywhere in docs | 2 |
| 2025-03-15 | notifications | Auth | Error message on expired API key says "403 Forbidden" with no guidance on renewal | 3 |
| 2025-03-18 | search | Auth | Same as 2025-03-15 | 3 |
| 2025-03-20 | ads-platform | Auth | Same expired-key issue — took 45 min to diagnose | 3 |

<!-- Auth/expired-key issue at 3x → platform sprint item #47 created -->

A single NPS score across all 30 teams tells the platform team almost nothing actionable. Segmenting responses by team size, use-case, and time-since-onboarding reveals which population is dissatisfied and why — which points directly at the root cause.

# scripts/analyse_survey.py
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("survey_responses.csv")

# Segment NPS by team use-case
use_case_nps = (
    df.groupby("use_case")
    .apply(lambda g: (
        (g["nps"] >= 9).sum() - (g["nps"] <= 6).sum()
    ) / len(g) * 100)
    .sort_values()
)
print("NPS by use case:")
print(use_case_nps.to_string())

# Identify teams with CSAT < 3.0 on any dimension
low_csat = df.groupby("team")[
    ["csat_onboarding", "csat_docs", "csat_support"]
].mean().lt(3.0).any(axis=1)
flagged_teams = low_csat[low_csat].index.tolist()
print(f"\nTeams with CSAT < 3.0 on any dimension: {flagged_teams}")

# Top pain points from open text (requires manual clustering first)
themes = df["top_pain_point"].value_counts()
print(f"\nTop pain point choices:\n{themes.to_string()}")

Teams in their first 30 days of Claude adoption consistently score onboarding 0.8 points lower than teams that have been using the platform for 6+ months. Segmenting by tenure is the most reliable way to distinguish onboarding problems (fixable with documentation and better tooling) from deeper platform dissatisfaction (requires architectural changes).
Connecting Survey Results to the Platform Roadmap
DevEx survey results must connect to concrete roadmap items — otherwise the feedback loop exists only on paper. The platform team allocates

to DevEx improvement items, and each quarterly survey must produce at least two sprint items that trace directly to a survey theme. The product owner writes the link explicitly in the sprint planning document.
Add 'Hello World in 5 minutes' tutorial to IDP
Add streaming example to SDK README + IDP

Running a quarterly DevEx survey manually — drafting, distributing, collecting, and analysing responses — takes 2–3 days of platform team time each quarter. Automating the distribution and aggregation pipeline reduces this to 2–3 hours, freeing the team to spend time on analysis and action rather than logistics.

The survey is sent via a Slack bot that DMs every engineer who is a member of a Claude-consuming team at the start of each quarter. The bot tracks who has responded (not what they responded) and sends a single reminder on day 7 to non-respondents. The survey closes on day 14.

# scripts/send_survey.py  — run at start of each quarter via cron
import os
from slack_sdk import WebClient
from survey_store import SurveyStore

SLACK_TOKEN = os.environ["SLACK_BOT_TOKEN"]
SURVEY_URL = os.environ["SURVEY_URL"]  # Typeform or internal form

client = WebClient(token=SLACK_TOKEN)
store = SurveyStore()

# Pull current quarter's Claude-consuming team members from IDP
members = get_claude_team_members()  # returns list of slack_user_ids

for user_id in members:
    if store.has_responded(user_id):
        continue
    client.chat_postMessage(
        channel=user_id,
        text=(
            f"Hi! The quarterly Claude Platform DevEx Survey is open. "
            f"It takes ~3 minutes and directly shapes our roadmap. "
            f"Your responses are anonymous (team-level only). "
            f"*<{SURVEY_URL}|Take the survey>*"
        ),
        unfurl_links=False,
    )

print(f"Survey sent to {len(members)} engineers.")

# scripts/aggregate_survey.py  — run after survey closes
import pandas as pd
from survey_store import SurveyStore
from slack_sdk import WebClient
import os

store = SurveyStore()
responses = store.get_all_responses()  # returns list of dicts

df = pd.DataFrame(responses)

# Calculate NPS
promoters = (df["nps"] >= 9).sum()
detractors = (df["nps"] <= 6).sum()
nps = round((promoters - detractors) / len(df) * 100)

# Segment CSAT averages by team
team_csat = df.groupby("team")[
    ["csat_onboarding", "csat_docs", "csat_support", "effort_score"]
].mean().round(2)

# Theme frequency
top_pain = df["top_pain_point"].value_counts()
open_text = df["open_text"].dropna().tolist()

report = {
    "response_count": len(df),
    "nps": nps,
    "team_csat": team_csat.to_dict(),
    "top_pain_points": top_pain.to_dict(),
    "open_text_samples": open_text[:20],  # top 20 for manual review
}

# Post summary to #platform-devex channel
client = WebClient(token=os.environ["SLACK_BOT_TOKEN"])
client.chat_postMessage(
    channel="#platform-devex",
    text=f"Q{quarter} DevEx Survey closed. NPS: {nps}. "
         f"{len(df)} responses. Full report: <link>",
)
Making Surveys Actionable: The Three-Threshold Rule
A common failure mode is running a DevEx survey, observing that NPS is 42, and not knowing whether that is good or bad or what to do about it. The
gives every metric a clear interpretation and a mandatory response at each level.

Red thresholds trigger an immediate retrospective with the platform Product Owner and engineering manager, not just a sprint item. The retrospective must produce a root-cause hypothesis and a 30-day remediation plan within one week of the survey closing.
quarterly is the maximum cadence for a comprehensive survey. More frequent = lower response rates and developer irritation.
if developers cannot see the connection between their feedback and platform changes, response rates drop to < 20% within two quarters.
an NPS of 42 across 30 teams may hide five teams at NPS –20. Always segment before concluding the platform is healthy.
even minor wording changes make quarter-over-quarter trends unreliable. Freeze question text after the first survey.
a friction log that nobody reviews becomes a graveyard of complaints. Assign explicit weekly review ownership with a 3-mention escalation rule.

DevEx surveys and feedback loops are the mechanism by which a Claude platform team converts developer frustration into a prioritised improvement backlog. The quarterly survey captures perception across five dimensions (NPS, onboarding CSAT, docs CSAT, support CSAT, effort score); the continuous friction log captures qualitative signal between surveys; telemetry captures behavioral data that perception surveys miss. Closing the loop — publishing 'You Said, We Did' and explicitly linking survey findings to sprint items — is what distinguishes a feedback loop from a data collection exercise.
Keep the survey to 7 questions maximum; use consistent wording quarter-over-quarter to enable trend analysis.
Segment results by team, tenure, and use-case — aggregate scores hide the populations most in need of help.
Publish 'You Said, We Did' within 30 days of survey close; name the themes and the actions taken.
Allocate 20% of each platform sprint to DevEx improvement items traced to survey findings.
Track the friction log weekly; three mentions of the same issue from different teams triggers a sprint item.
Reconnect the next survey to past pain points — ask whether the fixes actually landed.
